evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's annual birth numbers have collapsed from a peak of 93,000 in 2021 to 72,000 in 2025 (22% decline), with total fertility rate falling to 1.31 children per woman, the lowest in 14 years. Any cohorts entering school from 2028 onward will be materially smaller than cohorts a decade earlier, independent of special educational needs policy.

sources.title: Hungarian vital statistics and fertility data (2021-2025)

evidence_appendix.claim: The population of children aged 0-4 in Hungary is projected to decline by 5% between 2023 and 2033, representing a 'locked-in' demographic pipeline already born and distinct from future fertility uncertainty.

sources.title: EU population projections for Hungary (2023-2033)

evidence_appendix.claim: Between 2010 and 2020, students identified with special educational needs in Hungary rose from 6.7% to 7.7% in primary education and from 3.9% to 6.2% in secondary education. The drivers are contested: partly attributed to greater diagnostic awareness and improved diagnostics, and partly to social disparities and workforce shortages.

sources.title: Hungarian education statistics on SEN prevalence (2010-2020)

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2023 survey of 121 Hungarian primary teachers found that 83.6% reported an increase in students requiring developmental support, with teachers graduating in 2010 or later more likely to have encountered SEN students.

sources.title: Hungarian primary teacher survey on SEN (2023)

evidence_appendix.claim: Cross-national OECD evidence attributes the two- to fourfold increase in autism spectrum disorder diagnoses over 15 years largely to improved detection and diagnosis, not underlying prevalence increases. This suggests diagnostic practice change is a major driver across comparable healthcare systems.

sources.title: OECD cross-national analysis of autism spectrum disorder trends

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary's teaching workforce is aging and geographically concentrated: half of all teachers in 2024/2025 were aged 50 or older with only 7.2% under 30. Teacher shortages are greatest in rural areas, where the proportion of unqualified teachers in disadvantaged schools is 2-3 times higher than in non-disadvantaged schools.

sources.title: Hungarian teacher workforce demographics and regional distribution (2024/2025)

evidence_appendix.claim: A UN expert review linked Hungary's growing SEN cohorts to inadequate specialist support: 'the growing number of children with special education needs has not been matched by adequate teacher training or specialist support in Hungary; bigger classes in public schools pose significant challenges to accommodate SEN students and the shortage of specialists, such as doctors and sign language interpreters, further adds to the difficulties.'

sources.title: UN expert review of Hungary education system

evidence_appendix.claim: Unlike systems consolidating rural schools, Hungary historically responded to enrollment decline by reducing parallel class sections rather than closing schools, producing a large stock of small single-class-per-grade rural schools. This structural feature constrains how SEN/BTMN students can be operationally served, as single-class schools cannot separate pupils into parallel tracks.

sources.title: Historical analysis of Hungarian rural school responses to pupil decline

evidence_appendix.claim: Local government financing of Hungarian primary education deteriorated from central government covering 80-90% of costs in the 1990s to 50-70% (50% in small village schools) by the 2000s. This fiscal squeeze predates and is compounding current cohort decline, creating cumulative fiscal pressure independent of SEN identification trends.

sources.title: Analysis of Hungarian local education financing trends (1990s-2000s)

evidence_appendix.claim: Regional child-population trends in Hungary are spatially uneven: rural settlements in Western Transdanubia, Southern Baranya, and Northern Hungary experienced child-population contractions of 10-30% over a recent four-year period, while Northeastern counties (Borsod-Abaúj-Zemplén, Szabolcs-Szatmár-Bereg, Heves) show high disadvantage concentrations (70-92% child disadvantage rates in some settlements). North-South and East-West regional divides are pronounced.

sources.title: Regional analysis of Hungarian child-population trends and disadvantage concentration

evidence_appendix.claim: SNI student enrollment in Hungary has increased by 40% over the past 20 years, with 112,007 children registered in the 2024/2025 school year, representing nearly 10% of primary school students and over 11% by 8th grade

sources.title: KSH (Hungarian Central Statistical Office) data aggregated via Raabe.hu

evidence_appendix.claim: Autism spectrum disorder registrations among Hungarian children nearly tripled to quadrupled between 2013 and 2023/24, with conflicting source figures reporting either 3,319→9,737 or 3,061→11,685 depending on reference population

sources.title: KSH data cited in multiple education outlets

evidence_appendix.claim: Expert interpretation of rising autism registrations attributes increase primarily to diagnostic-practice change—improved detection methods, greater awareness, and earlier referral by parents and professionals—rather than true prevalence increase

sources.title: Education outlets citing expert commentary

evidence_appendix.claim: BTMN (behavioral/pedagogical needs category) prevalence in Hungary shows sharp age discontinuity: 2.1% of preschool children versus 9.5% of school-age children as of 2018/19, a fivefold difference suggesting school-entry academic/behavioral demands contribute substantially to identification

sources.title: Hungarian National Education Statistics 2018/2019

evidence_appendix.claim: The distinction between SNI and BTMN categories reflects severity and etiology: SNI learning disorders involve neurological/nervous-system deviation, while BTMN involves pedagogical and psychological factors without neurological deviation

sources.title: Hungarian education policy documentation

evidence_appendix.claim: Specialist assessment activity in pedagógiai szakszolgálat (educational counseling services) is increasingly consumed by BTMN/SNI expert-committee evaluations, crowding out direct therapeutic and remedial care time

sources.title: 2022 investigative account and service-staff reporting

evidence_appendix.claim: Special-education teacher training admission is high (2,944 first-choice applicants in 2024, ranking 8th nationally), but completion and workforce-entry rates are reported by union sources to be a small fraction (approximately one-fifth enter the profession, and fewer specialize in autism or state-sector roles)

sources.title: 2024 Higher-Education Admissions data and teachers' union commentary

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian school funding structure includes per-capita normative funding multipliers (2x to 3x) for SNI students, potentially creating fiscal incentive for enrollment-declining schools to classify students as SNI to offset revenue loss and avoid teacher dismissal or closure

sources.title: Pedagogical training document on education financing (studocu lecture notes, undated institutional source)

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian education has documented precedent for administrative/categorical mechanisms creating segregation pathways (e.g., 'Bridge classes' functioning as repositories for Roma pupils), establishing structural risk that SEN/BTMN labeling could similarly interact with school choice to produce segregation

sources.title: Kertesi & Kézdi segregation analysis and 2020 CEU working paper on social selection

evidence_appendix.claim: UN Special Rapporteur on the right to education (2025 country visit) identified special-needs student protection, curriculum assessment, segregation, and equitable funding as unresolved policy concerns in Hungary, warning of 'hollowing out' of public education and deepening inequalities

sources.title: UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) press release and Special Rapporteur country visit report

evidence_appendix.claim: Between 2020/21 and 2021/22 school years, recorded SEN students in Hungary rose from 92,800 to 97,300; by 2024/2025 the total reached 112,060, representing a 28,000-student increase over ten years, with varying baseline definitions across official reports (cross-level vs. primary-only totals).

sources.title: KSH Central Statistical Office data releases and Eduline reports, August 2022–May 2026

evidence_appendix.claim: The fastest-growing SEN subcategory is 'severe learning, attention, or behaviour-regulation disorder' (BTMN-adjacent), which comprised 45,300 students in 2021/22 (12% year-on-year increase) and approximately 69% of integrated SEN students, representing roughly 50% growth over ten years—compared to slower growth in sensory/motor/intellectual disabilities.

sources.title: KSH data reported in Népszava (August 2022) and Eduline (2022–2026)

evidence_appendix.claim: Official explanation attributes SEN growth 'partly' to modernization of diagnostic tools, but independent experts (clinical psychologist, teachers' union, 2025 reports) dispute that diagnostic modernization alone explains the magnitude; instead they attribute substantial causal weight to mandatory school enrollment from age six and stricter school-readiness assessments implemented in 2018, with acceleration post-2020.

sources.title: KSH statement; Népszava interview (August 2022); teachers' union source; Eduline (July 2026)

evidence_appendix.claim: SNI classification in Hungary functions primarily as a financing and service-eligibility category rather than strictly clinical diagnosis. Until 2013, institutions educating SNI students received higher per-capita normative state funding, creating potential institutional incentives to identify/register students; conversely, more recent reports suggest SNI status increasingly functions as an exclusionary rather than inclusive factor in admissions as institutional financing fails to support extra resources.

sources.title: Hungarian Wikipedia SNI entry (financing history); specialized pedagogical journal source (2020s)

evidence_appendix.claim: The specialist workforce shortage in Hungary is regionally concentrated (45 advertised gyógypedagógus positions in Budapest; 2–3 positions each in rural Transdanubian counties), yet enrollment in special-education degree programs rose 15% (to 3,768 first-choice state-scholarship applicants in 2026). However, many part-time correspondence-program students are parents of affected children unlikely to become practicing teachers, meaning rising enrollment does not directly map to future practicing workforce supply.

sources.title: Eduline (July 2026); special-education training program data

evidence_appendix.claim: 73% of SEN students (approximately 51,000) are educated in integrated (mainstream) settings rather than segregated special schools, yet institutions and specialists face 'increasingly large challenges' in providing integrated education. Teacher surveys report 83.6% of respondents observed increases in students requiring developmental support, and research indicates placement decisions are shaped as much by institutional resource capacity and structure as by student characteristics.

sources.title: Eurydice Hungary (July 2024); Education Sciences journal survey (2026)

evidence_appendix.claim: Established research from 2007–2020 documents that Roma students were overrepresented in SEN classification and segregated special-education classes in Hungary, with a threshold effect: in schools where Roma proportion is 50–75%, children are more likely to be tested and referred to segregated classes, whereas above 75% ethnic separation may occur outside the SEN classification system. Hungary's SEN integration policy has been comparatively more successful than the regional average, but this evidence is primarily historical and geographically local.

sources.title: Open Society Foundations monitoring (2007); ERRC research; qualitative studies cited in CEU working paper (2020)

evidence_appendix.claim: Free school choice in Hungary (instituted to allow students to enroll outside residential districts subject to capacity limits) is an independently documented driver of compositional school sorting. Kertesi and Kézdi research found that school segregation is most strongly predicted by the proportion of Roma students in a town, and that school-switching behavior significantly alters school ethnic composition, diminishing the role of residential distribution.

sources.title: Kertesi and Kézdi study reported in CEU news (2013); CEU CPS working paper (2020)

evidence_appendix.claim: Specialized Hungarian pedagogical scholarship identifies a 'domestic over-diagnosis' critique—that the number of children classified with learning disorders in Hungary is 'irrationally high'—and notes that international comparison with OECD countries is methodologically problematic because Hungary's diagnostic and service-provision system differs structurally in key respects and uses different definitional principles than OECD practice.

sources.title: Hungarian pedagogical journal source (2020s); folyoiratok.oh.gov.hu (Oktatási Hivatal journals)

evidence_appendix.claim: Rising SEN identification may change future educational trajectories and access (e.g., tracking into vocational streams, secondary-school access, higher-education pathways) through changes in SNI status from historical advantage to current frequent exclusion in admissions, but longitudinal quantification of these distributional outcomes is absent from retrieved sources and represents a priority evidence gap.

sources.title: Contextual references in specialized pedagogical sources; inference from earlier findings

evidence_appendix.claim: Finland's 2010–2011 education reform introduced a three-tiered support system (general, intensified, special) as a direct response to rising special education identification, restructuring provision from a two-tier to a three-tier non-categorical model.

sources.title: Finnish education system special education reform documentation

evidence_appendix.claim: Post-reform identification growth in Finland concentrated in the new 'intensified support' tier (rising from 2.8% in 2011 to 12.2% by 2020), while 'special support' (top tier) remained flat at approximately 8.5–9.0%, suggesting reclassification across a newly created category rather than uniform acceleration.

sources.title: Finnish comprehensive school support statistics 2011–2020

evidence_appendix.claim: Non-census-based special education funding systems correlate with increases in SEN provision, whereas census-based systems remove fiscal incentives to identify students as having SEN; Finland's 2010 funding reform shifted toward census-based allocation and reduced full-time special-education placement.

sources.title: Pulkkinen et al. 2020 – Special education funding reform and identification rates

evidence_appendix.claim: Large and persistent regional/municipal variation in special support provision remained after Finland's 2010 reform, with regional rates ranging from 4% to 13% of comprehensive-school pupils, suggesting that national identification figures mask locally driven differences in practice and administrative interpretation.

sources.title: Finnish regional equity analysis in special needs education provision

evidence_appendix.claim: Only 23% of Finland's Tier 3 (special support) students are fully included in general education classrooms; a majority receive special education in special classes, creating a gap between headline inclusion-tier percentages and actual mainstream integration rates.

sources.title: Finnish comprehensive school placement patterns and Tier 3 inclusion analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: Teachers in Finland's tiered support system reported positive overall assessment but identified challenges including extended documentation burdens, insufficient time to support all pupils in mixed classes, and in some municipalities, cost-saving motives driving tier assignment rather than pedagogical rationale.

sources.title: Teacher perspectives on Finnish three-tiered support system implementation

evidence_appendix.claim: Finland's historical equity gains from 1970s de-tracking reforms have partially reversed since approximately 2000, coinciding with rising school choice and selective classes, though causal attribution is contested across multiple co-occurring systemic changes.

sources.title: PISA trend analysis and Finnish school system equity evolution 1970–2020

evidence_appendix.claim: Parental and school choice in Finland enables selective sorting by prior student performance, with 'capable' (typically higher-SES) parents selecting into selective classes, leading to performance-based school fragmentation and concentration of disadvantage in non-selective schools.

sources.title: Finnish school choice and selective classes segregation research

evidence_appendix.claim: A documented Finnish study explicitly examines the intersection between pupils' support-tier level and school intake/enrolment under choice-based systems ('The dilemma of inclusion and school choice — pupils' support-tier level and lower-secondary schools' intake in Turku', Lempinen, Berisha, & Seppänen 2016), indicating that SEN status may interact with school choice outcomes.

sources.title: Lempinen, Berisha, & Seppänen 2016 – Inclusion and school choice in Turku

evidence_appendix.claim: Rising socioeconomic and ethnic residential segregation in Finnish cities (with over one-third of Finnish school children in urban areas) transmits into schools through residential-assignment and school-choice mechanisms, placing municipalities in a structural tension between market-enrolment rules and equality imperatives.

sources.title: Nordic urban segregation and school-choice system interaction analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: The number of children identified as SNI (special educational needs) in Hungary grew from 92,800 to 97,300 in a single year (2021/2022), representing the largest year-on-year increase in at least 12 years, and the 2023/2024 figure reached approximately 63,000 or 8.8% of primary-school pupils, with a 40% rise over the preceding 20 years.

sources.title: Central Statistical Office (KSH) data reported by eduline.hu and Világgazdaság

evidence_appendix.claim: Composition of SNI growth is uneven: severe learning disorders increased 12% in 2021/2022 alone (45,300 children); boys are classified SNI at 11% versus girls at 6.1%; and county-level variation ranges from 5.4% to 14%, a 2.6-fold spread far larger than plausible true prevalence differences.

sources.title: KSH data reported by eduline.hu and education sector sources

evidence_appendix.claim: BTMN (integration, learning, behavioral difficulty) is a distinct Hungarian legal category defined in Act CXC of 2011, legally milder than SNI, with 2.1% prevalence among preschool children (2018/2019) but 9.5% among school-age children—a nearly five-fold jump attributed by sources to either genuine emergence of difficulties at school entry or differential screening sensitivity between settings.

sources.title: Act CXC of 2011; pedagogiai szakszolgálat definition and KSH statistics (2018/2019 school year)

evidence_appendix.claim: The expert committee system (pedagógiai szakszolgálat) operates as a sole nationwide gatekeeper for both SNI determination and BTMN designation, with authority to recommend integrated versus segregated placement, but this gatekeeping structure has not been restructured despite rising caseloads and documented waiting lists of several months for diagnostic assessment.

sources.title: Act CXC of 2011 on National Public Education; pedagogiai szakszolgálat operational structure; HVG (2022) manifesto from specialist professionals

evidence_appendix.claim: A shortage of special-education teachers (gyógypedagógus) exists with the largest concentration of unfilled positions in Budapest (45 advertised positions) and unequal regional distribution, while applications to the state-subsidized special-education degree program rose 15% from 2025 to 2026 cohorts (from 3,270 to 3,768 first-choice applicants).

sources.title: eduline.hu (2024, 2026) workforce reporting

evidence_appendix.claim: The December 2023 anti-segregation law does not address free parental school choice under the voucher-financing model (Act Nkt.) or the selective six- and eight-grade gimnázium tracks, which critics argue remain the structural channels through which segregation strategies operate independent of SEN identification policy.

sources.title: December 2023 anti-segregation law (Hungarian government); critical analysis by Mérce citing Együttnevelés Szövetség and Tanítanék Mozgalom

evidence_appendix.claim: European Court of Human Rights rulings in Horváth and Kiss v. Hungary (2013) and Szolcsán v. Hungary (ongoing) found discrimination in segregation of Roma children and called for safeguards against misclassification; an ongoing EU Commission infringement procedure addresses systemic segregation, yet Hungary has not provided requested data on whether new diagnostic procedures have reduced misclassification of Roma students.

sources.title: ECtHR case law (Horváth and Kiss v. Hungary 2013; Szolcsán v. Hungary); European Commission infringement procedure (March 2023)

evidence_appendix.claim: A tightening of school-readiness rules from January 2020, which restricted school-start deferral and required six-year-olds to enter school rather than remain in kindergarten, is cited in sources as one plausible non-exclusive explanation for part of the observed SNI growth, implying that policy changes in adjacent domains (school-entry age) can measurably affect SEN identification without intent to change SEN policy itself.

sources.title: Government school-readiness rule change (2020); analysis in education sector sources

evidence_appendix.claim: NGO advocacy sources estimate that two-thirds of Roma children in Hungary are taught separately in segregated schools, classes, or special institutions, and that a large proportion of Roma children are misclassified as mildly intellectually disabled, making this a major mechanism of educational segregation, yet these figures are unaudited advocacy estimates rather than official statistics.

sources.title: Rosa Parks Foundation advocacy materials; ERRC documentation

evidence_appendix.claim: The documented rise in SNI identification in Hungary (cited as 40% growth over 20 years) lacks a single reconciled public statistical series by consistent definition across years and education levels, creating a monitoring and feedback-loop gap for policymakers.

sources.title: Research Notes: SEN Identification Trends and Implementation Capacity in Hungary

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2017–2018 reform removed exemption from grading and narrative assessment for BTMN (but not SNI) students, creating a documented incentive mechanism for reclassification toward SNI, but expert opinion is sharply contested on whether this materially increased SNI numbers.

sources.title: National Public Education Act Amendment (Hungary)

evidence_appendix.claim: Diagnostic/assessment services (Pedagógiai szakszolgálat) report capacity constraints including multi-month waiting lists, staff turnover, and insufficient medical backup, creating a documented bottleneck early in the identify-assess-serve sequence.

sources.title: Practitioners' petition on psychiatric and pedagogical service capacity (2022)

evidence_appendix.claim: Once assessed, children frequently do not receive the legally prescribed habilitation and therapy hours specified in expert opinions, indicating a second-stage implementation failure distinct from diagnosis.

sources.title: Research Notes: SEN Identification Trends and Implementation Capacity in Hungary

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2013 centralization of counseling services under state management (KLIK) was followed by a documented shift in time allocation toward assessment/paperwork and away from therapeutic service provision, representing an organizational-design driver of capacity misallocation.

sources.title: Historical account of Pedagógiai szakszolgálat centralization (2013)

evidence_appendix.claim: Per-capita supplementary grants for SNI students constitute only a weak financial incentive to reduce learning disadvantage in the absence of strict requirements tied to local educational programs, and foundation schools may use SNI status as a funding consideration despite reversed assessment-admission sequencing.

sources.title: Public-finance research on Hungarian per-pupil funding; parent-guidance source on foundation schools

evidence_appendix.claim: Established economic research (Kertesi–Kézdi) demonstrates that Hungary's free-school-choice regime combined with residential/commuting mobility drives school segregation and cross-boundary sorting by family status, establishing a pre-existing structural condition into which rising SNI identification enters.

sources.title: Kertesi–Kézdi research on Hungarian school segregation and free choice

evidence_appendix.claim: Mainstream school leaders informally self-limit SNI intake based on perceived institutional capacity, a safeguard-like behavior that exists at the school level but is not centrally monitored, audited uniformly, or documented in policy.

sources.title: Management-science dissertation on integrated SNI education in Hungarian mainstream primary schools

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary has experienced a documented rise in SEN identification, with a fivefold increase in one diagnostic sub-category (psychological developmental disorders) over a five-year period, and significant county-level variation in identification rates

sources.title: European Agency for Special Needs and Inclusive Education - Hungary Country Profile

evidence_appendix.claim: Rising SEN identification in Hungary is partly driven by definitional and categorization changes rather than solely by changes in underlying prevalence, as Hungary moved from an 'organic versus non-organic' dichotomy that did not accurately link diagnoses to actual service needs

sources.title: European Agency for Special Needs and Inclusive Education - Hungary Country Profile

evidence_appendix.claim: Across OECD countries, autism spectrum disorder diagnoses have increased two to fourfold over 15 years, with OECD evidence indicating this rise is largely attributable to improved detection and diagnostic practice rather than increased underlying autism prevalence

sources.title: OECD Analysis of Autism Spectrum Disorder Trends Across Member Countries

evidence_appendix.claim: In England, Education, Health and Care Plan (EHCP) awards rose 71% between 2018 and 2024 (from 180,000 to 252,000), with the Institute for Fiscal Studies identifying finance-design mechanisms—ring-fenced funding blocks, frozen cash-terms thresholds, and EHCP-gated top-up funding—as plausible drivers alongside expanded recognition of need

sources.title: Institute for Fiscal Studies Analysis of English EHCP Growth

evidence_appendix.claim: In England, regions with higher special education funding levels award more EHCPs and show higher proportions of children in segregated special schools, suggesting funding-linked allocation rather than purely needs-based assessment

sources.title: Lee et al. (2024) Cohort Study of Children with Genetic Differences and EHCP Awards

evidence_appendix.claim: Wales' switch from categorical SEN definitions to a non-categorical Additional Learning Needs (ALN) framework resulted in measurable identification decline to 11.2%, compared to 13.6% in England, 18.2% in Northern Ireland, and 37% in Scotland, demonstrating high sensitivity of identification rates to definitional architecture

sources.title: UK Four-Nation Comparison of SEN/ALN Identification Rates (Wales, England, Scotland, Northern Ireland)

evidence_appendix.claim: Finland's tiered support model (general, intensified, and special support) decouples formal SEN identification from access to educational support, with 31% of students receiving support at one tier or another but no requirement that children be labeled as having SEN to receive help

sources.title: Finnish Basic Education Act Reforms and Three-Tiered Support System Documentation

evidence_appendix.claim: In OECD countries, needs-based allocation of education entitlements (typical in most systems) differs structurally from diagnosis-gated allocation (as in Israel), with diagnosis-gated systems theorized to create stronger incentives toward identification but this distinction is not directly tested across systems

sources.title: OECD 11-Country Review of Autism Support Allocation Mechanisms

evidence_appendix.claim: In Hungary and Central Europe, Roma children are disproportionately placed in special schools and diagnosed with SEN through documented practices including ethnic-based diagnostic discrimination, with estimates suggesting one in ten Roma children still placed in special or segregated SEN provision, reflecting institutional racism compounding educational segregation

sources.title: European Agency and Central European Regional Research on Roma SEN Overrepresentation and Segregation

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian mainstream classrooms typically comprise 24 children with one teacher for all subjects and one specialist monitoring progress across four years, lacking the dual-teacher and dedicated special education teacher (SET) co-teaching infrastructure central to Finland's tiered support model implementation

sources.title: Hungarian Education Classroom Structure Documentation and Finnish System Infrastructure Comparison

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian SNI population grew continuously from 2012/13 onwards, with documented acceleration from 2021/22: KSH data report 112,007 SNI children in 2022/23 (up from 107,421 prior year) and 104,865 in 2023/24 (up 5,277 from prior year), representing approximately 40% growth over 20 years.

sources.title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office (KSH) compiled by Telex and follow-up reporting

evidence_appendix.claim: The 2017–18 'lex Taigetosz' reform (amendment to Public Education Act, effective September 2018) ended exemption from grading/classification for BTMN students while retaining it for SNI students, restructuring the legal incentive between the two categories.

sources.title: Public Education Act 2017 amendment; TASZ civil-liberties objection; 2024 Telex analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: SNI status is established exclusively by county-level (megyei/fővárosi) expert committees under EMMI decree 15/2013, not by medical diagnosis alone. BTMN status is established by district-level (járási) committees, which cannot establish SNI. Medical diagnosis does not confer legal SNI entitlement; an educational-administrative expert committee decision is required separately.

sources.title: EMMI decree 15/2013 (pedagógiai szakszolgálati intézmények); Hungarian education administration structure

evidence_appendix.claim: Formal appeal/review of SNI assessment exists but is time-limited (15 days to initiate review under EMMI 15/2013 §39(10)) and channeled through administrative (not judicial) procedures. Expert-committee reviews are conducted ex officio upon first educational year following implementation or upon parental/authority/institutional request.

sources.title: EMMI decree 15/2013 §39(10); Hungarian administrative review procedures

evidence_appendix.claim: SNI funding architecture ties per-pupil financing to classification: under task-based financing (feladatfinanszírozás), SNI pupils count as 2–3 pupils (depending on classification) for calculating teacher-post subsidies. This mechanism could theoretically incentivize over-identification, though no causal evidence links this financial structure to the documented rise.

sources.title: Hungarian normative financing reform (224,000 HUF/pupil baseline in 2012/13) to task-based system; education-finance analysis

evidence_appendix.claim: Special-education teacher (gyógypedagógus) shortages are geographically acute and coincide with historic low in overall public-education teacher numbers: several northeastern districts employ no special-education teachers despite rising SNI-identified children; total public-education/vocational-training teacher count fell ~1,600 from 2022/23 to 2023/24, reaching its lowest level since c. 2000.

sources.title: Academic analysis of special-education workforce; Hungarian Central Statistical Office employment data

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary has decade-plus unresolved legal accountability on discriminatory misclassification of Roma children: ECtHR *Horváth and Kiss v. Hungary* (2013) found ethnic discrimination in SNI/remedial-school placement; *Szolcsán v. Hungary* (2023) required national policy to end segregation in education; Committee of Ministers monitoring implementation reports unresolved case; European Commission 2016 infringement proceedings status remains opaque.

sources.title: ECtHR Horváth and Kiss v. Hungary (2013); ECtHR Szolcsán v. Hungary (2023); Committee of Ministers implementation monitoring; European Commission infringement proceedings (2016)

evidence_appendix.claim: 2023–24 UN and Ombudsman data corroborate continuing ethnic-segregation issues: ~35% of Roma students attend majority-Roma schools; segregation complaints constituted 69% of Ombudsman for Fundamental Rights complaints (2023); systemic over- and misdiagnosis of Roma children with learning disabilities identified as major concern; Hungary does not systematically collect ethnically-disaggregated data on segregation patterns.

sources.title: UN Special Rapporteur report (2023–24); Hungarian Ombudsman for Fundamental Rights (2023); Rosa Parks Foundation study (2024)

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2016–2017 structural reorganization reversed the 1999 reform direction by replacing 6-year elementary schools with 8-year primary schools, abolishing lower secondary schools (gymnasiums), and replacing 3-year lower secondary schools with 4-year secondary schools

sources.title: Polish education system structural reform (2016–2017)

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2016–2017 reorganization explicitly included rollback of special needs mainstreaming policies, reducing inclusion of students with special needs in mainstream classes

sources.title: World Class Learning Systems synthesis of Polish education reform

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland funds special educational needs support through per-pupil disability weightings ranging from 1.4 for mild intellectual disability to 9.5 for profound intellectual disability, with autism diagnoses unlocking subsidies up to approximately 9,500 zł compared to 1,400 zł for mild disability

sources.title: Polish SEN financing mechanism and weighted subsidies

evidence_appendix.claim: As of 1 January 2025, Poland replaced its 'subwencja oświatowa' with 'kwota potrzeb oświatowych' algorithm using per-pupil base standard (9,477 zł in 2025) multiplied by disability weights; the subvention for disabled pupils increased 14% from 2023 to 2024 (6,899 zł to 7,866 zł), independent of prevalence changes

sources.title: Polish education financing algorithm reform 2025

evidence_appendix.claim: Diagnostic authority for special education status in Poland is centralized in Psychological and Pedagogical Counselling Centres and education superintendents, not vested in school headmasters or teachers; this creates a gatekeeping structure separate from schools

sources.title: Polish SEN diagnostic authority structure

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2016–2017 structural reform was implemented rapidly against documented objections from educational researchers, local authorities, parents, and teachers, without extended consensus-building

sources.title: Polish education reform implementation and stakeholder responses

evidence_appendix.claim: Implementation of Poland's gymnasium phase-out produced a double-cohort crunch in 2017, with both gymnasium graduates and primary school graduates competing for high school places; in Warsaw alone, 3,173 students did not secure high school placement, leading to unplanned technical school enrollment

sources.title: Polish structural reform implementation crisis (2017)

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's reported special educational needs identification for autism increased 30% in a single year (2022/2023 school year to 82,199 children), with regional increases exceeding 1,000% in some provinces, and the male-to-female diagnostic ratio declined from 4.56 (2012) to 2.99 (2023), suggesting improved recognition of autism in females

sources.title: Polish autism and SEN identification trends (2022–2023)

evidence_appendix.claim: Poland's 2019 teacher strike involving almost 80% of schools was partially motivated by frustration over implementation chaos from the 2016–2017 structural reform, alongside poor working conditions and salaries

sources.title: Polish teacher strikes and education system discontent (2019)

evidence_appendix.claim: Whether Poland's reversal of 1999–2015 comprehensive education reforms contributed to the large PISA 2022 decline remains contested and unresolved; alternative explanations include persistence of reforms in teacher autonomy and family beliefs about tertiary attainment that were not reversed

sources.title: Polish PISA decline and education reform sequencing (speculative)

evidence_appendix.claim: Diagnosis of special educational needs in Hungary is legally gatekept by expert committees (szakértői bizottságok) operating at county or district level, and parental consent is required to initiate investigation in all cases.

sources.title: Act CXC of 2011 on National Public Education

evidence_appendix.claim: Since 2012–13, the state centralized diagnostic and support infrastructure from municipalities to regional pedagogical assistance services, removing local authority control over assessment networks.

sources.title: Act CXC of 2011 on National Public Education - implementation September 2012

evidence_appendix.claim: More than 60% of participating Hungarian schools reported needing additional special education teachers, psychologists, or pedagogical assistants; the number of such specialist staff doubled from 2010 to approximately 2023, but geographic distribution remains uneven, with one county reporting an average of 39.6 students per special education teacher.

sources.title: EU Country Fiche - Hungary Education Sector

evidence_appendix.claim: The European Court of Human Rights issued a judgment against Hungary in March 2023 for racial segregation of Romani children in education, finding violation of right to education and non-discrimination; Hungary was ordered to desegregate and pay damages. As of 2024, the Committee of Ministers monitoring implementation reports the case remains open due to insufficient Hungarian government compliance.

sources.title: European Court of Human Rights judgment - Szolcsán v. Hungary (March 2023); Committee of Ministers monitoring records

evidence_appendix.claim: In July 2023, the Hungarian government enacted a unified legal status law for teachers across all provider types (state, church, private, foundation schools) without adequate union consultation, restricting strike rights and granting employers unilateral authority to reassign educators to different schools to address teacher shortages.

sources.title: Hungarian Parliament Status Law bill (4 July 2023); PSZ/PDSZ union statements

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungarian schools have discretion to admit students from outside their catchment area, creating asymmetric ability to include or exclude depending on institutional incentive; this mechanism has been exercised to exclude Roma students seeking transfer to non-segregated schools.

sources.title: Szolcsán v. Hungary litigation record and Hungarian school admissions law

evidence_appendix.claim: The European Commission opened an infringement procedure against Hungary in 2016 for segregation of Romani children in schools, naming segregated church schools as a contributing factor. As of 2024, this procedure remains formally open with no public resolution announced.

sources.title: European Commission Infringement Procedure (2016); EC Directorate-General for Education

evidence_appendix.claim: The EU suspended €32 billion in funding to Hungary but released €10.2 billion in cohesion funds in December 2023 following judicial reforms. Multiple observers criticized the release decision as politically transactional, coinciding with Budapest's withdrawal of a veto on Ukraine aid, and the Commission has not deployed sector-specific SEN/education conditionality despite the open infringement procedure.

sources.title: European Commission fund management announcements (December 2023); European Parliament reports; Hungarian civil society monitoring

evidence_appendix.claim: Between 2010 and 2020, primary education SEN identification increased from 6.7% to 7.7%, and secondary education rose from 3.9% to 6.2%; separately, absolute numbers increased 1.6-fold over 20 years, but these metrics use different reference periods and sources remain unreconciled into a single harmonized time series.

sources.title: Hungarian Central Statistical Office and EU education statistics

evidence_appendix.claim: A 2003 amendment extending SEN categories to include autism and learning difficulties from psychiatric developmental disorders was followed by a fivefold increase in learners classified under this new category within five years, with significant inter-county variation, prompting a full population review and rule amendment in 2007.

sources.title: Act LXXIX of 1993 on Public Education (2003 amendment) and subsequent 2007 diagnostic review

evidence_appendix.claim: Teacher shortage in Hungary is driven by low pay (among Europe's lowest), the highest student-teacher ratio, and insufficient support staff; Hungary committed to raising average teacher salary to 80% of tertiary-educated worker salary by 2025 and achieved a 21.2% average raise from January 2025, but no source quantifies whether this addresses specialist (as opposed to generalist classroom-teacher) shortages.

sources.title: EU 2022 country report on Hungary; Hungarian government recovery and resilience plan and EFOP Plus programme; January 2025 government decree

evidence_appendix.claim: UN Special Rapporteur Farida Shaheed (2025 country visit) assessed that the growing number of children with SEN has not been matched by adequate teacher training or specialist support, with larger classes and documented shortages of specialists such as doctors and sign language interpreters presenting barriers to accommodation.

sources.title: UN Special Rapporteur on the right to education – country visit report, Hungary 2025

evidence_appendix.claim: In segregated schools where Romani students exceed 75%, institutional segregation may prevent SEN expert assessment referrals, whereas in schools with 50–75% Romani pupils, children are more likely to be tested, diagnosed with SEN, and referred to segregated special classes; concurrently, socially deprived and uneducated parents often lack awareness of procedural rights during assessments without support personnel.

sources.title: 2024 field research on segregation and SEN classification patterns in Hungarian schools

evidence_appendix.claim: Historical ombudsman findings and litigation documented schools unlawfully applying for supplementary ethnic funding while operating segregated classes, and academic literature describes segregated education as incentivized by state funding mechanisms; however, whether identical finance incentives operate under current (post-2020) funding rules remains unestablished.

sources.title: Hungarian ombudsman findings and case law summaries; academic literature on segregated education finance

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary has transformed former special institutions into unified special educational/conductive-pedagogical resource centres (EGYMI) since the early 2000s, providing both special education and integrative services to support mainstream teacher capacity for inclusive practice; this non-structural support infrastructure predates the current identification rise and was designed for integration rather than segregation.

sources.title: Hungarian Ministry of Education special educational services documentation; policy literature on EGYMI establishment

evidence_appendix.claim: Research documents that deterioration of the public school system led more affluent middle-class parents to lose trust in state institutions and opt for private or denominational schools, with the number of church-run educational institutions doubling since 2010; a 2025 UN assessment warns this private-school growth will leave public schools with fewer resources and higher concentrations of disadvantaged students, increasing segregation.

sources.title: Qualitative research on parental school choice; UN assessment 2025; OECD/IEA data on school composition trends

evidence_appendix.claim: Academic explanations attribute the current SEN identification rise partly to greater awareness of developmental disorders and improved diagnostics leading to earlier, more accurate diagnoses, though this interpretive claim derives from qualitative interview research (21 teachers in one underdeveloped eastern region) and lacks population-level causal tests distinguishing awareness/diagnostic-practice effects from true prevalence change or incentive-driven over-identification.

sources.title: Qualitative research interviews with teachers on diagnostic awareness and practice changes

evidence_appendix.claim: The number of children identified with special educational needs (SNI) in Hungary exceeded 69,000 in the 2025/2026 school year according to KSH (Central Statistical Office) data, with a broader count across all education levels reaching nearly 120,000.

sources.title: KSH (Central Statistical Office) Helyzetkép survey and public education statistics

evidence_appendix.claim: Recorded SEN prevalence varies dramatically by county, ranging from 15.6% of students in Békés county to 6.2% in Borsod-Abaúj-Zemplén county.

sources.title: Regional education statistics cited in SEN research

evidence_appendix.claim: The January 2020 tightening of school-readiness assessment and deferral rules resulted in deferral applications falling sharply to 16,400 by 2021, and KSH time-series data show SNI numbers rising markedly faster from 2021/2022 onward.

sources.title: Szülői Hang advocacy analysis and KSH time-series synthesis

evidence_appendix.claim: Northeast Hungary had no special-education-teacher (gyógypedagógus) training program until 2017, and a 2009 early-intervention study documented substantial regional inequalities in diagnostic and service access, with particularly weak services and long waiting lists in Northern Hungary regions.

sources.title: Special-education workforce development records and 2009 early-intervention system study

evidence_appendix.claim: Currently 4,900 special-education/conductor-role teachers work in general schools, plus 949 developmental teachers and 1,404 specialists in the traveling special-education-teacher and conductor network (approximately 7,253 total specialist positions nationally).

sources.title: Official staffing counts from educational administration

evidence_appendix.claim: SNI-status children and up to two accompanying persons are entitled to free intercity travel to educational institutions, but travel-support entitlements are contingent on receiving a voucher issued by an Expert Committee following successful SEN assessment.

sources.title: Hungarian education policy on SNI student transport entitlements

evidence_appendix.claim: Traveling specialist-education networks show wide variance in coverage scale and capacity, ranging from networks serving 30+ institutions with 200+ children to regional networks serving 50+ institutions with 350+ children, with some networks unable to provide category-specific specialist care (e.g., sensory disabilities) locally.

sources.title: Regional traveling-specialist network operational reports

evidence_appendix.claim: Children from multiply disadvantaged families are significantly more likely to have developmental problems remain unidentified or, if identified, to not receive development services; additionally, official SEN figures cover only children with formal expert opinions, implying an unquantified population of children with unmet SEN who lack formal diagnosis or assessment.

sources.title: 2023 disadvantage and SEN identification study; advocacy-sector assessment estimates

evidence_appendix.claim: Hungary exhibits extreme school segregation in small settlements, with disadvantaged-pupil concentration ranging from 20% to 80% within the same locality, shaped by covert admissions practices, church-school choice, and parental mobility responses to changing school composition.

sources.title: School segregation research on Hungarian education systems

content.generation_notice: Fresh live v2 production replicate using the admitted lens registry. Generator=anthropic; judge=openai; human external-use gate remains pending.
content.public_question: More children are being identified as having special educational needs — what should Hungary do?
content.summary: Hungary's rising special-educational-needs (SEN/SNI/BTMN) identification—registered numbers have climbed roughly 40% over twenty years, reaching 112,060–112,007 by 2024/25 with sharp acceleration after 2021/22 [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01][F-live-legal_and_governance-01][F-live-hungarian_education_system-01]—presents six distinguishable transformation paths, none a clear winner. A passive baseline (T1) treats current drift as a counterfactual: it changes nothing in gatekeeping, category definitions, or finance, but leaves demographic contraction (0-4 population down 5% by 2033 [F-live-demography-02], births falling 22% since 2021 [F-live-demography-01]) interacting with a static, aging, unevenly distributed workforce [F-live-demography-06], unresolved ECtHR/EU obligations [F-live-legal_and_governance-09][F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-09], and county disparities (5.4%-14% [F-live-hungarian_education_system-02]) to continue unaddressed—most lenses judge this not fiscally, legally, or equity-neutral even though it requires no new coalition.　　Measurement reform (T2) is the lowest-risk, foundational option: it would reconcile divergent series (92,800→97,300 vs 112,060 vs 69,000 [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01][F-live-school_network_planning-01]) and decompose true prevalence from diagnostic-practice, definitional, and incentive effects, using natural experiments like the 2018 BTMN grading-exemption change [F-live-implementation_planning-02] and the 2020 school-readiness rule tightening, which is directly linked to accelerated SNI growth [F-live-hungarian_education_system-08][F-live-school_network_planning-04]. Comparative and Finnish evidence supports this decomposition logic strongly (OECD attributes most autism-diagnosis growth to detection improvements [F-live-demography-05][F-live-international_comparison-03]; Finland's post-2010 growth concentrated in one new tier while the top tier stayed flat [F-live-finnish_reform-02]). Its limitation: better data does not itself change access or resource allocation.　　Structural/categorical redesign (T3) targets the sole nationwide expert-committee gatekeeper and its documented multi-month waiting lists [F-live-hungarian_education_system-04], drawing on Wales' non-categorical ALN transition (11.2% vs England 13.6%, Scotland 37% [F-live-international_comparison-06]) and Finland's tiered model [F-live-international_comparison-07]. But Finland's persistent 4%-13% municipal variation after reform [F-live-finnish_reform-04] and its dependence on dual-teacher/SET infrastructure Hungary lacks [F-live-international_comparison-10] limit transferability, and Poland's rapid, non-consensual 2016-2017 restructuring produced severe disruption (3,173 Warsaw students without placement, an 80%-school strike [F-live-polish_reform-07][F-live-polish_reform-09]) and even rolled back mainstreaming [F-live-polish_reform-02].　　Finance redesign (T4) would shift per-pupil multipliers (2x-3x for SNI [F-live-education_finance-08][F-live-legal_and_governance-07]) toward census-based allocation, but no causal evidence ties Hungary's specific financing mechanism to its identification rise, and Poland's contrasting expansion of disability-weighted funding (weights 1.4-9.5, subventions rising 14% independent of prevalence [F-live-polish_reform-03][F-live-polish_reform-04]) while separately centralizing gatekeeping [F-live-polish_reform-05] weakens the universality of the incentive theory, though Finland's census-shift did coincide with reduced full-time placement [F-live-finnish_reform-03].　　Workforce expansion (T5) is the most politically uncontroversial and directly evidenced option, targeting acute regional shortages (Budapest's 45 unfilled posts versus 2-3 in rural counties [F-live-hungarian_education_system-05][F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05], districts with zero specialists [F-live-legal_and_governance-08]), but rising training enrollment does not reliably convert to practicing supply [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05][F-live-education_finance-07], and general salary reform (21.2% raise [F-live-portuguese_reform-03]) may not reach specialist shortages specifically.　　Anti-segregation/adjacent-policy coordination (T6) most directly confronts free school choice and selective gimnázium tracks left unaddressed by the 2023 law [F-live-hungarian_education_system-06], responding to unresolved Horváth and Kiss and Szolcsán judgments and the 2016 EU infringement procedure [F-live-hungarian_education_system-07][F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-09], and Kertesi-Kézdi evidence that school-switching independently reshapes ethnic composition [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08]. It faces the highest political resistance of all six options, given a decade of documented non-compliance [F-live-political_feasibility-10] and contested NGO estimates of segregation's scale [F-live-hungarian_education_system-09].　　Decision-makers must choose between empirically weak but politically easy levers (T1, T5) and evidence-richer but disruptive or resistance-heavy structural levers (T3, T6), while T2 and T4 sit as enabling or contested intermediate steps. The research most likely to reshape this choice concerns the causal decomposition of identification drivers, the fiscal-incentive test for Hungary specifically, and whether tiered reform would relabel rather than newly serve need.
content.title: Rising Special Educational Needs Identification in Hungary: Framing the Policy Problem
